dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding to a priority one for possible stroke or chest pain at a residence in [CITY] due to an unconscious or near-fainting call.
Hi, I'm Kim, a paramedic with the rescue squad, and this is my partner, Jenny, an EMT. Can you tell us what happened here?
We found her on the couch - completely unresponsive.
Was she breathing when you found her?
Yes, she was breathing, but she didn't move.
She doesn't appear to be responsive. I'll assess for AVPU to determine her level of consciousness.
We tried shaking her and calling her name - no response.
No eye opening on command or painful stimulus.
No verbal response.
No motor response observed. Her motor score is 1.
Airway is patent and there is no sign of obstruction.
Her respiratory rate is 14 - within normal limits so far.
Respiratory effort and depth look normal.
SpO2 is 99% on room air.
Pulse is 80 beats per minute and regular.
Blood pressure is 133 over 79. She's sitting up.
Glucose is 119 - this is normal.
We'll need some personal info. Could you tell me her name and approximate age?
Her name is Ruth, and she's about 58 years old.
When was she last known normal?
About one hour ago, she was fine - no issues reported.
Any important medical history or surgeries?
She has had a heart attack, bypass surgery, and a stroke, I think. But the family can't say for sure about details.
Do you know about any drug allergies?
Yes, she's allergic to penicillin.
She didn't report any pain, but what symptoms did she have once she started waking up?
She mentioned tingling on the left side and difficulty lifting her left leg.
I'll check for any skin abnormalities - no rashes or bruising observed.
Pupils equal, round at 3mm, and reactive. Her neck is soft with no stiffness.
No signs of obstruction or discharge in the ears, nose, or mouth.
Performing a 12-lead EKG as part of our stroke protocol.
EKG reading is sinus rhythm. Initial interpretation suggests anterior infarct findings.
She's regaining awareness and showing mild left-sided facial asymmetry.
Using the VAN scale and BEFAST stroke assessment - signs of left-sided weakness are present.
Beginning continuous ECG monitoring as required by protocol.
Activating the stroke/TIA alert protocol. Contacting [COMPANY_NAME] immediately.
Confirming current pulse at 78 and respiratory rate 18.
BP remains at 119 over 73 - still acceptable for her age.
SpO2 remains 99% on room air.
Second blood glucose level is 0 - could be a technical error.
Attempting IV access but she is a difficult stick - will need to consider other methods.
She reports using a nitroglycerin spray earlier but had no chest pain after that.
No direct pain complaints now, but she's indicating left-sided discomfort post-awakening.
Given the stroke alert, EKG findings, and symptoms - direct transport to [COMPANY_NAME] with a hot alert.
She's being moved to the stretcher with a two-person carry - placed in semi-Fowler's position with a five-point harness.
She's been transferred to the CT table using a sheet drag and handed over at the bedside.
Remember the first glucose is 119 - this zero might not reflect accurate data.
We'll wait until she's stable and recheck glucose if needed.
Replace the test strip, re-cap bubble if present, and retest when she's more immobilized.
Acknowledged. We'll re-evaluate once transported, if possible.
She's back to normal glucose and no new signs of pain. Left-side weakness remains a concern.
She's speaking more clearly - the tingling seems gone, but left-side weakness is still persistent.
Rechecking skin for new bruising or signs of vascular compromise - no changes noted.
No active bleeding, jaundice, or injection sites observed.
She's now oriented and more alert. Pupils remain 3mm and reactive.
No active obstruction, trauma, or drainage observed.
Bilateral breath sounds, no wheeze or crackles upon auscultation.
A second 12-lead EKG is consistent with the initial findings. Noting Q-waves in V1 and T-wave changes.
These findings are consistent with a prior anterior infarct. Review is being done by the receiving facility.
A stroke alert has been initiated - patient is en route for direct imaging at the hospital.
Vital signs stable - pulse 78, SpO2 99%.
Neurology has been alerted. Transporting with hospital prepped for early imaging.
Hospital imaging gown is ready. Ready for mobilization.
She appears asymptomatic except for left-sided deficits. No medicine is being administered now.
Her past medical history includes hypertension and CABG surgery - typical for stroke and MI risk.
Current meds: antiplatelet agent, beta blocker, metformin. No insulin used - important for hospital.
Previous stroke affects her current neurological status and explains the left-side asymmetry.
Family is unaware of prior stents placement - only stated MI and bypass.
No major changes noted. Vitals remain stable and acceptable for transport.
She remains oriented and aware. Facial asymmetry and left-side weakness are still present.
No further drug administration planned unless signs progress. Will re-check glucose only if movement returns.
[COMPANY_NAME] is prepared. Hospital is on standby. Transporting by ambulance with communication ahead.
She is secured on the stretcher in semi-Fowler's position. Harnesses, documents, and meds are organized.
Transport vitals are within normal and being held steady.
Will repeat glucose testing only if mobility or symptoms arise in the field.
Initial reading likely due to movement, but a known baseline glucose of 119 is intact.
Transport status remains hot - [COMPANY_NAME] has been notified. Emergency imaging is the immediate next step.
Directing transport from field to CT. Ensuring bed availability at arrival point.
Update communicated ahead. Vitals, meds, and EKG notes on board for handover.
Patient remains stable. No emergent signs expected subsequent to field review.
The second glucose is unnecessary unless clinically indicated or movement stabilizes.
Medical equipment secured to stretcher for uninterrupted transport. No changes in condition anticipated.
Final call-out to [COMPANY_NAME] - Ruth is en route, field vitals stable, with acute neurological findings.
All transport meds, notes, and personal items are at the head of the stretcher and documented.
[COMPANY_NAME] Mobil declared. Prepping for direct CT unit entry upon arrival.
RN at CT scanner position is aware of arrival. Direct handover anticipated.
Stroke/TIA alert submitted. No chest pain reported post-nitro.
Patient aware and handled without incident.
Patient keeps responsive but has persistent left-sided symptoms.
Neurological defects maintain but no drop in respiratory function per field review.
No clear indicators of post-episode reaction or escalation with transportation media.
Transportation tolerant - no change direction or need for paused transport.
Rechecking IV site. Previous site failed - will replace with clean dressing.
Notes, Field findings, and drugs transmission complete at onset of transport.
Full report communicated - identification, presentation, and field history at arrival site.
Handover occurring at approaching CT table - anticipates hospital verification of findings.
Field involvement concluded. Care is now transitioning to hospital team.
No field complications noted. Action items agenda completed and submitted.
Baseline glucose 119. No signs of instability. No further checks to be done.
Transport finalized without incident. Under hospital watch now.
All EMS procedures completed and patient status stable.
Transport complete. Transition to hospital care ongoing.
Prior cardiac and stroke history noted - all relevant documentation submitted.
Transported to CT. No more field treatment necessary at this stage.
Handover complete at bedside. No deficiencies in patient care upon transfer.
Transported efficiently with no events.
Patient was transferred safely and assessed stable post-transfer.
No additional data to add from this experience.
Baseline and second test readings are consistent - no clinical concern.
Transport action completed. Case filed and reviewed at the end of shift.
